coredata.dc:title: Tumor microenvironment responsive CD-CuO2 nanohybrids for imaging-guided self-enhanced chemodynamic therapy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ruihan
Hao, Juanyuan
coredata.dc:description: Copper peroxide (CuO2) nanoparticles have attracted considerable interest in tumor chemodynamic therapy (CDT) recently. However, specific excipients are usually needed to improve their stability and dispersibility. Here, tumor microenvironment responsive CD-CuO2 nanohybrids were designed via a facile one-pot approach, in which carbon quantum dots (CDs) were employed as fluorescence-activatable stabilizers, achieving stabilization of ultrafine CuO2 nanoparticles through a tumor-specific fluorescence activation mechanism. The optimized CD-CuO2 nanohybrids with 19.45 % Cu content and an average hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of ∼150 nm, demonstrating desirable stability with quenched fluorescence in the simulated blood medium containing glutathione (10 μM) and vitamin C (6 μg/mL), while decomposing to release Cu2+ and H2O2 for self-enhanced CDT as well as fluorescent CDs for imaging in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment. The in vitro cellular uptake demonstrated that the proposed CD-CuO2 nanohybrids could be internalized by the HepG2 cells and disintegrated therein. Cellular studies confirmed efficient internalization and on-site disintegration within HepG2 cells, exhibiting tumor-specific CDT efficacy (IC50 = 27 μg/mL) while showing negligible cytotoxicity toward normal L02 cells (IC50 = 16 mg/mL), demonstrating significant tumor selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tumor chemodynamic therapy
Imaging-guided
Self-enhanced
CD-CuO2 nanohybrids
Tumor-specific nanotheranostics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CD-CuO2 nanohybrids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a) and DLS result (b) of CDs; DLS result (c) and TEM image (d) of CD-CuO2 nanohybrids; Zeta potential of CDs and CD-CuO2 nanohybrids (data are mean values ± SD, n = 3) (e); Raman spectrum of CD-CuO2 nanohybrids (f); XRD patterns of CDs, CD-Cu2+, CD-Cu(OH)2, and CD-CuO2 nanohybrids (g); XPS survey spectra, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Cu 2p spectra (h–l) of CD-CuO2 nanohybrids.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs in pH 7.4 PBS (a), the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids after treating with pH 7.4 PBS containing VC (6 μg/mL) and GSH (10 μM) (b) and pH 5.0 ABS containing GSH (10 mM) (c) for different times (λex = 360 nm).

Dh of CD-CuO2 nanohybrids fabricated at different Cu:CDs ratios (data are mean values ± SD, n = 3) (a); FL intensities of the products at different process steps (data are mean values ± SD, n = 3) (b); Dh of the optimized CD-CuO2 nanohybrids in PBS (pH = 7.4) with different concentrations at 0–48 h (data are mean values ± SD, n = 3) (c); stability of the optimized CD-CuO2 nanohybrids and corresponding CD-Cu2+ nanoassemblies in pH 7.4 PBS containing VC (6 μg/mL) and GSH (10 μM) (data are mean values ± SD, n = 3) (d); DLS results of the CD-Cu2+ (e) and CD-CuO2 (f) in pH 7.4 PBS containing VC (6 μg/mL) and GSH (10 μM) for 30 min; CD-CuO2 nanohybrids before and after treating with pH 5.0 ABS (g) and pH 5.0 ABS containing GSH (10 mM) (h) for 48 h.

The concentration of the released H2O2 in ABS (pH = 5.0) and PBS (pH = 6.5/7.4) with CD-CuO2 (200 μg/mL) at different times (a) and in ABS (pH = 5.0) with different CD-CuO2 concentrations (25, 50, 100, 200, and 500 μg/mL) for 15 min (data are mean values ± SD, n = 3) (b).

UV–Vis spectra of TMB in PBS (pH = 5.0) with H2O2 (50 mM) and CD-CuO2 (200 μg/mL) and both (a); CD-CuO2 and H2O2 in different acidities (b); with different CD-CuO2 concentrations and at pH 5.0 with H2O2 (50 mM) (c) and incubation with CD-CuO2 (200 μg/mL) and H2O2 (50 mM) for different times (d).

IFM images of the HepG2 cells after incubated with CD-CuO2 for 24 h: (a) DAPI, (b) CDs, and (c) merge. Cell viability assay after treated with CDs (d) and CD-CuO2 nanohybrids (e) for 24 h (data are mean values ± SD, n = 3, Statistical significances were calculated via Student's t-test: ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, and ∗∗∗p < 0.001).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CD-CuO2 nanohybrids (a) and their tumor intracellular fluorescent imaging-guided self-enhanced chemodynamic therapy mechanism (b).

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This article is part of a special issue entitled: ICSM2024 published in Materials Today Chemistry.

head.ce:title: Tumor microenvironment responsive CD-CuO2 nanohybrids for imaging-guided self-enhanced chemodynamic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper peroxide (CuO2) nanoparticles have attracted considerable interest in tumor chemodynamic therapy (CDT) recently. However, specific excipients are usually needed to improve their stability and dispersibility. Here, tumor microenvironment responsive CD-CuO2 nanohybrids were designed via a facile one-pot approach, in which carbon quantum dots (CDs) were employed as fluorescence-activatable stabilizers, achieving stabilization of ultrafine CuO2 nanoparticles through a tumor-specific fluorescence activation mechanism. The optimized CD-CuO2 nanohybrids with 19.45 % Cu content and an average hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of ∼150 nm, demonstrating desirable stability with quenched fluorescence in the simulated blood medium containing glutathione (10 μM) and vitamin C (6 μg/mL), while decomposing to release Cu2+ and H2O2 for self-enhanced CDT as well as fluorescent CDs for imaging in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment. The in vitro cellular uptake demonstrated that the proposed CD-CuO2 nanohybrids could be internalized by the HepG2 cells and disintegrated therein. Cellular studies confirmed efficient internalization and on-site disintegration within HepG2 cells, exhibiting tumor-specific CDT efficacy (IC50 = 27 μg/mL) while showing negligible cytotoxicity toward normal L02 cells (IC50 = 16 mg/mL), demonstrating significant tumor selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemodynamic therapy (CDT) has been extensively investigated as a promising tumor-specific ROS-based therapeutic modality, owing to its high tumor microenvironment (TME) selectivity [1,2]. This approach leverages localized metal-catalyzed Fenton reaction or Fenton-like reaction to convert less reactive hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) into highly toxic ⋅OH radicals without external energy input, enabling dual therapeutic advantages: selective tumor cell membrane/DNA damage and minimized off-target toxicity. However, the efficacy of CDT in TME is still hampered by inefficient ROS generation with the classic Fe-catalyzed Fenton reaction due to the strict catalytic condition (pH 2–4) of Fenton reaction and limited endogenous H2O2 content [3,4]. As an attractive alternative, the Cu-based Fenton-like reaction has been explored with an efficiency of 160-fold higher than that of Fe-based system under mild pH condition [5,6], although the copper-based Fenton reagents are still limited due to their poor stability and systemic toxicity risks from copper leakage [7,8].

Despite the higher selectivity and efficiency, the practical clinical application of CDT is still severely restricted by insufficient oxy-substrates supply in tumor microenvironments (50–100 μM of H2O2), as well as the higher GSH level which would consume the generated •OH. To provide sufficient exogenous H2O2 and highly active catalyst, copper peroxide (CuO2) has been exploited as the first example of a Fenton-type metal peroxide nanoparticle for tumor-specific H2O2 self-supplying CDT [9]. Compared with the other H2O2 self-supplying CDT strategies using catalase or catalase-like substances, more cupper catalyst could be provided with CuO2, besides the H2O2 self-supplying capacity. By now, various CuO2-based nanocomposites have been reported for the enhanced CDT by avoiding it decomposition and renal clearance during blood circulation for a precise tumor targeted delivery with optimized therapeutic outcomes and minimized side effects [10], by improving CuO2 stability with inert supports or excipients, such as natural [11] or synthetic polymers [12–17], silica [18–22], MOFs [23,24], COFs [25], CaCO3 [26] or Ca3(PO4)2 [27], and so on.

Owing to the excellent off-on fluorescence of the carbon quantum dots (CDs) via quenching their fluorescence by complexing with Cu2+ ions and pH/glutathione (GSH) dual-triggered decomposition of the resultant complex, the resultant CD-Cu2+ nanoassemblies have been designed as tumor-specific nanotheranostics for the fluorescent imaging-guided Cu-mediated CDT [28–31]. However, these complexes suffer from their poor stability due to the chelation interaction. To improve the stability and further endow the H2O2 self-supplying capacity, the tumor microenvironment responsive CD-CuO2 nanohybrids were designed via a facile one-pot method as potential nanotheranostics for imaging-guided self-enhanced chemodynamic therapy in the present work, with CDs as tumor-specific turn-on fluorescent glue to stabilize the ultra-fine CuO2 nanoparticles (Scheme 1 a). The proposed CD-CuO2 nanohybrids were quite stable in the simulated blood medium with low level of GSH and VC, while decomposing in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment to release Cu2+, H2O2, and fluorescent CDs. Therein, the released Cu2+ ions transformed into Cu + ions for the Fenton-like reaction of H2O2 by oxidation GSH to GSSG, which could also up-regulate the intracellular H2O2 level for a high-performance CDT. Meantime, the fluorescent CDs accumulated in the cellular nuclei for fluorescence imaging (Scheme 1b). Thus, tumor-specific imaging-guided self-enhanced CDT was achieved with an IC50 of 27 μg/mL on the HepG2 cells, as a model tumor cell line.

The tumor microenvironment responsive CD-CuO2 nanohybrids were fabricated via a facile one-pot approach, as illustrated in Scheme 1a. The biocompatible fluorescent CDs were synthesized via a microwave-assisted (MW-assisted) pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine, as reported previously [32].

CD-Cu2+ nanoassemblies were typically prepared by adding 1.0 mL of copper acetate solution with different concentrations into 14.0 mL of an aqueous CD dispersion (containing 30.0 mg CDs) under ultrasonication (US) at the room temperature. As for the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids, 1.0 M NaOH solution (at twice the molar amount of copper acetate) was added under ultrasonication. Subsequently, 1.0 mL of H2O2 (30 %) was added dropwise, and the mixture was stirred for 1 h in an ice bath. The resulting CD-CuO2 nanohybrids were collected by centrifugation (3000 rpm, 5 min), washing with water for three times, and finally lyophilization.

The stability of the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids was evaluated by the DLS analysis of their aqueous dispersions at a concentration of 20 μg/mL after treating with different media for different times, in comparison with the CD-Cu2+ nanoassemblies at the same concentration.
The fluorescence emission was measured on a fluorescence spectrometry at excitation of 360 nm, after treating the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids with different media for different folds of 10 μg/mL.

The H2O2 release was detected via the ammonium molybdate method [33], which measured the characteristic UV absorbance of peroxomolybdate complex at 350 nm. Briefly, CD-CuO2 aqueous dispersions were prepared in various buffer solutions at different concentrations. After 100 μL of the dispersion was added into 5.0 mL of 2.4 mM (NH4)2MoO4 aqueous solution, the mixture was shaken for certain time before the UV–vis detection at 350 nm. The effect of medium pH values was investigated at pH 5.0 acetate buffer solution (ABS), pH 6.5 phosphoric acid buffer solution (PBS), and pH 7.4 PBS at a CD-CuO2 concentration of 200 μg/mL after shaking for 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10, and 15 min. The effect of the CD-CuO2 concentrations was investigated at pH 5.0 ABS after shaking for 15 min at a concentration of 25, 50, 100, 200, and 500 μg/mL. A standard curve was calculated using known concentrations of H2O2 solutions.

The ·OH generation was detected via the 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl-benzidine (TMB) method by recording the absorbance at 652 nm, as reported previously [21,23]. Briefly, the UV absorbance at 652 nm was measured after adding 1.0 mL of the CD-CuO2 dispersion in pH 7.4 PBS into 4.0 mL of 0.25 mg/mL TMB solution in different buffers for different times. The effect of medium pH values was investigated at pH 5.0, 6.5, and 7.4 using 200 μg/mL CD-CuO2 nanohybrids after 15 min incubation. The effect of the CD-CuO2 concentrations was investigated at pH 5.0 for 15 min at a concentration of 0, 25, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL. The effect of time was investigated with 200 μg/mL of the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids at pH 5.0 for 0, 5, 10, 15, and 20 min.

L02 and HepG2 cells were incubated with DMEM containing fetal bovine serum (10 %) in an environment of 37 °C and 5 % CO2. For cellular uptake, the HepG2 cells were observed and analyzed by using inverted fluorescence microscope, after co-culturing with CD-CuO2 nanohybrids (10 μg/mL) for 24 h and stained with 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI). As for the cytotoxicity, the cell viability was detected by methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay after co-incubation with CD-CuO2 nanohybrids at different concentration for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CD-CuO2 nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatible fluorescent N-doped CDs were synthesized via the microwave-assisted pyrolysis method [32], with spherical shape of approximately of 2.8 nm (Fig. 1 a). A Dh of 3.5 nm with narrow hydrodynamic diameter distribution was achieved in the DLS analysis (Fig. 1b), due to swelling. In the fluorescence analysis, the CDs exhibited excitation-independent emission with the maximum excitation length of 360 nm (Fig. 2 a).

Then the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids were fabricated via a facile one-pot method with different Cu:CDs ratios (Scheme 1). Increasing the Cu:CDs ratio from 2:1 to 20:1, the Dh of the resultant CD-CuO2 nanohybrids initially decreased and then increased (Figs. 3a and 1 c and S1). Fluorescence analysis confirmed the successful stepwise transformation in this one-pot synthesis: from free CDs to CD-Cu2+ nanoassemblies, then to CD-Cu(OH)2 nanohybrids, and finally to CD-CuO2 nanohybrids (Fig. 3b and S2). After adding Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence of CDs was partially quenched due to the chelating interaction [34]. Further quenching occurred after transforming Cu2+ ions into Cu(OH)2, attributed to the deprotonation of the amino groups on the CDs by adding NaOH, besides the static quenching by complexation. Subsequent conversion to CuO2 by adding H2O2, lead to near-complete fluorescence quenching. This result from the compact aggregation of the CDs interconnected with the formed ultrafine CuO2 nanoparticles, regardless of the Cu:CDs ratios. The quenched fluorescence of the CDs in the proposed CD-CuO2 nanohybrids is expected to be recovered by the acid/GSH dual-triggered decomposition in the tumor cells, for a turn-on fluorescence imaging, accompanying the self-enhanced CDT by the released Cu2+ ions and H2O2 after decomposition.

For a better passive targeting via enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, the one fabricated at the Cu:CDs ratio of 4:1 (Dh ~ 150 nm) was selected for the further investigation (Fig. 1c). Different from the uniformly spherical shape of the CD-Cu2+ nanoassemblies [30], the proposed CD-CuO2 nanohybrids show an irregular cluster-shaped morphology in the TEM observation (Fig. 1d), maybe due to the damage of the chelation structure by adding NaOH. The damage also endowed the negatively charged surface with Zeta potential near to the pristine CDs for an excellent stability in blood circulation (Fig. 1e).

The in-situ formation of CuO2 was revealed by the Raman, XRD, and XPS analysis. Besides the D band and G band of CDs at 1373 and 1571 cm−1, the characteristic absorbance of Cu–O and O–O bonds appeared at 617 and 820 cm−1 in the Raman analysis (Fig. 1f). In the XRD analysis (Fig. 1g), the CD-Cu2+ nanoassemblies show a broad peak at 2θ around 23°, similar as the pristine CDs, indicating the amorphous graphitic carbon in CDs [32]. After adding NaOH, the diffraction peaks of the orthorhombic structure of Cu(OH)2 appeared [35], while those of CuO2 appeared at 32° and 38° after adding H2O2 [36], demonstrating the transformation of Cu2+ ions to Cu(OH)2 and CuO2 in the proposed one-pot approach. In the HR-TEM observation, the loose cluster-shaped morphology was revealed with homogeneously dispersed C, N, O, and Cu elements (Fig. S3), further revealing the successful fabrication of the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids with CDs as the glue to stabilize the ultra-fine CuO2 nanoparticles.

The Cu 2p signal appears at 934 eV in the survey XPS spectrum of the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids, besides the signals of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s at 285, 399, and 531 eV (Fig. 1h). The high-resolution C (1s) and N (1s) XPS spectra revealed the successful synthesis of the N-doped CDs (Fig. 1i and j), while the high-resolution O (1s) and Cu (2p) XPS spectra indicated the successful formation of CuO2, which was interacted with the CDs via Cu–O bond [21].

In the present work, CDs were used as tumor-specific turn-on fluorescent glue to stabilize the ultra-fine CuO2 nanoparticles. To testify the stabilization, the optimized CD-CuO2 nanohybrids were treated with pH 7.4 PBS for different times. As presented in Fig. 3c and S4, the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids remain the constant Dh even after 48 h, demonstrating their excellent dispersibility in PBS, owing to their stable structure and negatively charged surface.

Considering the GSH and VC in the actual blood circulation, the stability of the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids was investigated by treating with pH 7.4 PBS containing VC (6 μg/mL) and GSH (10 μM). Distinctly different from the fast decomposition of the CD-Cu2+ nanoassemblies within 20 min, the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids maintained their Dh around 150 nm after 30 min (Fig. 3d–f and Fig. S5). The results indicated the excellent stability of the proposed CD-CuO2 nanohybrids in the possible practical application for CDT. Moreover, the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids could gradually disintegrate into fragments without pristine CDs released in the acidic intracellular environment (Fig. 3g and Fig. S6), and the decomposition was accelerated in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment with a high GSH level, in which they could disintegrate completely into CDs within 48 h (Fig. 3h and Fig. S6). Such feature makes the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids a promising candidate for the tumor-specific imaging and self-enhanced CDT.

In the fluorescence analysis of the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids after treating with different media for various times, very slight increase in the fluorescence intensity can be found after treating with the pH 7.4 PBS containing VC (6 μg/mL) and GSH (10 μM) mimicking the blood medium (Fig. 2b), while a significant increase in the fluorescence intensity was achieved in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment (pH 5.0 ABS containing GSH (10 mM)) (Fig. 2c), indicating the recovery of the fluorescent CDs in the acid/GSH dual-triggered decomposition of the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids in the tumor cells. Such turn-on fluorescence emission was expected to endow the in-situ real-time imaging diagnosis for a desired imaging-guided self-enhanced CDT.

The H2O2 release was revealed by the ammonium molybdate method, as reported previously [33], calculated with the calibration curve obtained with H2O2 aqueous solutions (Fig. S7). The CD-CuO2 nanohybrids did not release H2O2 in the simulated blood medium, while H2O2 could be released in the simulated extracellular medium with a weak acidic pH value of 6.5, especially in the intracellular microenvironment with a higher acidity (pH = 5.0) (Fig. 4 a). Moreover, the H2O2 concentration increased with increasing the dose of the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids (Fig. 4b). These results demonstrated that the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids could acid-triggered release H2O2 in the extracellular and intracellular media, providing a H2O2 self-supplying capacity for a potential self-enhanced CDT.

Then the ⋅OH generation capacity was evaluated by the TMB method [21,23]. A weak absorbance of oxTMB could be seen after adding the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids into the TMB solution at pH 5.0, while there is no absorbance by adding H2O2 (Fig. 5 a). The results demonstrate the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids can decompose to release both Cu2+ ions and H2O2 for the Fenton-like oxidation of TMB, as a H2O2 self-supplying system [11,12,14,17,20–23]. Specially, the H2O2 self-supplying capacity could only function in the extracellular and intracellular media but not the blood circulation (Fig. 5b), demonstrating their safety to the normal cells and tissue. In presence of an exogenous H2O2 at a concentration of 50 mM, the absorbance of oxTMB increased with increasing the CD-CuO2 concentration (Fig. 5c) or prolonging the treating time (Fig. 5d), revealing the excellent Fenton-like catalytic activity of the released Cu2+ ions. All the results indicated that the proposed CD-CuO2 nanohybrids could be used as H2O2 self-supplying system for the self-enhanced CDT.

Finally, the potential application of the proposed CD-CuO2 nanohybrids for the tumor-specific imaging-guided self-enhanced CDT was assessed in vitro. After incubation the HepG2 cells in presence of the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids (10 μg/mL) for 24 h, the fluorescence of CDs (green channel) can be seen overlapping with the nuclei (blue channel) stained with DAPI (Fig. 6 a–c), indicating the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids could be internalized into the HepG2 cells and therein decomposed to release the fluorescent CDs [30], which could be used for the tumor imaging.

The inverted fluorescence microscope (IFM) images revealed the successful internalization and decomposition of the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids in the HepG2 cells. Accompanying the release of the fluorescent CDs during the decomposition of the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids, Cu2+ ions and H2O2 could also be released, which were expected for a self-enhanced CDT on the tumor cells. To reveal the point, the cell viability of the HepG2 cells and L02 cells were measured via the MTT technique after incubation in presence of the pristine CDs and CD-CuO2 nanohybrids at different concentrations for different times. As depicted in Fig. 6d and e, the cell viabilities of both cell lines were above 85 % even with a high dose of 40 μg/mL of the pristine CDs, demonstrating the excellent cytocompatibility of the pristine CDs.

As for the CD-CuO2 nanohybrids, the cell viability of the HepG2 cells and L02 cells decreased with increasing the dose from 2.5 to 40 μg/mL, showing a dose-dependent cytotoxicity on both cell lines (Fig. 6e). However, the cell viability of the L02 cells is much higher than that of the HepG2 cells at the same dose of CD-CuO2 nanohybrids. At the dose of 40 μg/mL, the cell viability of the L02 cells remained above 80 %, while that of the HepG2 cells was close to 40 %. The IC50 was calculated as 27 μg/mL and 16 mg/mL on the HepG2 cells and L02 cells respectively, indicating a tumor-specific CDT of the proposed CD-CuO2 nanohybrids with suppressed toxic side effect on the normal cells.

In summary, biocompatible N-doped CDs were used as tumor-specific turn-on fluorescent glue to fabricate the tumor microenvironment responsive CD-CuO2 nanohybrids as potential nanotheranostics for imaging-guided H2O2 self-supplying self-enhanced CDT via a facile one-pot approach. The proposed CD-CuO2 nanohybrids were stable with turn-off fluorescence in the simulated blood medium containing GSH and VC, meaning the successful stabilization of the ultra-fine CuO2 with CDs. They could be acid/GSH dual-triggered decomposed in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment, releasing Cu2+ and H2O2 for self-enhanced CDT as well as fluorescent CDs for imaging. The optimized CD-CuO2 nanohybrids with Cu content of 19.45 % and Dh around 150 nm could be internalized by the HepG2 cells and decomposed therein. As a result, tumor-specific imaging-guided H2O2 self-supplying self-enhanced CDT was achieved with IC50 of 27 μg/mL on the tumor cells, but no obvious cytotoxicity on the normal cells. The facile fabrication, excellent tumor microenvironment responsive decomposition and H2O2 self-supplying capacity make the proposed CD-CuO2 nanohybrids potential nanotheranostics for imaging-guided self-enhanced CDT in future tumor treatment.

Ruihan Liu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Juanyuan Hao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52272147).
